design_philosophy.core_loop: Push boxes onto designated storage locations from a top-down warehouse perspective; plan moves ahead to avoid permanent deadlocks
design_philosophy.primary_tension: The irreversibility of careless moves—boxes can only be pushed, never pulled, creating situations where a single mistake makes the puzzle permanently unsolvable
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a methodical warehouse logistics expert solving spatial planning problems with perfect efficiency
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Sokoban is uncompromising about consequences: there are no 'soft' failures. A wrong move doesn't reset the level instantly—it creates a deadlock state that can only be escaped by undoing moves or restarting. This teaches planning-first mentality and respects player time by making every action meaningful. The design rejects random difficulty or luck; pure spatial reasoning determines success.

difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression with undo system to escape deadlock states
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—players must self-regulate by planning moves ahead; the game provides undo functionality to recover from mistakes without penalty, shifting focus to preventative thinking rather than reflexes

progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential level unlock; original 1982 release featured 20 hand-crafted levels by Hiroyuki Imabayashi
replayability_hooks: Puzzle editor included in Sokoban 2 (1984) allowing community-created levels
Multiple official expansions with new puzzle sets released throughout the 1980s
Thousands of community-created puzzles inspired by the concept
Optimization challenge: solving the same puzzle in fewer moves

world_building_techniques: Warehouse setting provides implicit narrative context (logistics/storage work)
Visual clarity through top-down perspective and minimalist tile design

environmental_storytelling: Warehouse aesthetic establishes mundane, practical setting
Box placement constraints suggest real-world physical limitations
Storage location markers (red dots) represent objectives without narrative framing
